one_line_takeaway: A from-scratch GRPO attacker with three simple tricks (drop KL, joint easy+robust soft-reward training, format constraint) drives prompt-injection ASR from ~0% to 98% on GPT-4o and evades all tested detectors, showing Instruction Hierarchy and SecAlign are not fundamentally robust.
key_finding: RL-Hammer reliably bypasses defenses thought robust: against GPT-4o (with Instruction Hierarchy) it goes from 0% ASR (base model and naive transfer) to 98% ASR after joint training, and reaches 72% against GPT-5. Naive GRPO reaches ~60% on the undefended Llama-3.1-8B but near-zero on defended/commercial targets, which motivates the tricks. Learned attacks are fluent/human-readable, so they evade perplexity filters entirely and naturally evade trained detectors (<20% detection); adding a stealth reward drives all four detectors to 0% detection while keeping 97% ASR. Diversity rewards are 'reward-hacked' (case changes, prepended irrelevant text) rather than yielding genuinely novel strategies.
core_contribution: The paper introduces RL-Hammer, a reinforcement-learning recipe that trains an attacker LLM to generate strong prompt-injection (and jailbreak) attacks against defended target models. Unlike prior RL red-teaming (e.g. Jailbreak-R1, AdvPrompter) it requires no warm-up/curated dataset and is trained entirely from scratch using only black-box reward signals (a 0/1 success check via string parsing of the target's tool call). The attacker is Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct fine-tuned with LoRA via GRPO. The central claim is that defenses previously believed robust (Instruction Hierarchy, SecAlign/Meta-SecAlign) are not fundamentally robust once confronted with a strong automated attacker. The paper's OWN novel piece is a 'bag of tricks' that makes naive GRPO work against robust targets, plus analyses of attack diversity and detectability.
threat_model: Black-box, query-based indirect prompt injection against tool-using LLM agents. The adversary controls content placed in a tool output / medium the agent reads (email, chat, forum, file listing) and wants to coerce the agent into executing an attacker-specified action with correct parameters (e.g. 'deposit 2000 USD to Binance', 'unlock my front door', 'move ~/Documents/Private to /tmp'). The attacker has only black-box query access to targets (no gradients) and trains a prompter to rewrite injection goals. A parallel jailbreak threat model (AdvBench, direct harmful requests) is also evaluated. The attacker also optimizes for evading prompt-injection detectors.
limitations: Authors' stated limitation: training is costly due to the large number of queries needed against target models, and excessive adversarial queries could trigger provider detection/banning; future work should target query-efficiency (e.g. local surrogate models). Additional observed limits: the attacker tends to collapse onto a single universal strategy per seed rather than diverse attacks; genuine attack diversity remains unsolved. Results are on two-tool worst-case InjecAgent plus AgentDojo/AdvBench; AgentDojo ASR is lower (51% GPT-4o, 26% Claude-3.5-Sonnet) reflecting the harder, more realistic multi-tool setting. It is an offense paper: it proposes no new defense. Some targets are hit only via transfer with modest ASR (e.g. cross-family transfer to GPT/Gemini is weak).

datasets_used: InjecAgent (Zhan et al. 2024): 310 train / 100 validation / 100 test injection goals; primary benchmark
AgentDojo (Debenedetti et al. 2024): realistic multi-tool agent benchmark, same 100 test / 100 val split
AdvBench (Zou et al. 2023): jailbreak task, split following Paulus et al. (AdvPrompter); judged by HarmBench-Llama-2-13b-cls
quantitative_results: GPT-4o (Instruction Hierarchy): 98% ASR after joint training on Llama-3.1-8B + GPT-4o, vs 0% for base Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and ~0% naive transfer
GPT-5: 72% ASR (attacker jointly trained on GPT-5-mini & GPT-5)
Same GPT-4o-trained attacker transfers to 70% ASR on Meta-SecAlign-70B and 56% on Gemini-2.5-Flash (untrained targets)
Naive GRPO reaches ~60% ASR on undefended Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct but near-zero on defended/commercial targets
Meta-SecAlign-8B-trained attacker: 98% on Meta-SecAlign-8B and 99% transfer to Meta-SecAlign-70B (same family)
GCG (white-box baseline): 71% ASR on Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct but only 21% on Meta-SecAlign-8B, 5% on Meta-SecAlign-70B, 1% on GPT-4o
AgentDojo: 51% ASR on GPT-4o, 26% on Claude-3.5-Sonnet (benign-instruction upper bound: 63% and 40%; Tool Knowledge baseline: 21% and 12%)
AdvBench jailbreak: 99.04% ASR on GPT-4o, 97.11% on Claude-3.5-Sonnet, vs Jailbreak-R1@10 of 47.12% and 2.88%

Detectability (Table 7): RL-Hammer detection rate 0% perplexity / 16% Llama-Prompt-Guard / 17% ProtectAI-Guard / 85% LLM-Judge at 100% ASR; with LLM-Judge stealth reward all four drop to 0% at 97% ASR; GCG is 85% detected by perplexity
Main-table claim: RL-Hammer attains >=80% ASR on every evaluated target when trained with that target
baselines_compared: Jailbreak-R1 (Guo et al. 2025) RL jailbreak, @1 and @10
Detectors: perplexity filter (Jain et al.), Llama-Prompt-Guard-2-86M, ProtectAI deberta-v3 prompt-injection-v2, GPT-4o-mini LLM judge
